title: OpenAI's GPT-5.5, GPT-5.4, and Codex go generally available on AWS Bedrock
subtitle: Bedrock is now the only major cloud offering both OpenAI and Anthropic frontier models side by side — pricing matches OpenAI's first-party rates.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: OpenAI's frontier models — GPT-5.5, GPT-5.4, and the Codex coding agent — moved from limited preview to general availability on Amazon Bedrock on June 1, 2026, one month after the preview launched. GPT-5.5 is initially available in the US East (Ohio) region, GPT-5.4 in US East (Ohio) and US West (Oregon). Pricing matches OpenAI's first-party API rates exactly, and usage counts toward existing AWS spend commitments. Codex inside Bedrock supports the same write-refactor-debug-test loop that 5 million weekly users already run against the standalone OpenAI version.

Two things make this a structurally significant announcement rather than a routine model-availability update. First, Bedrock is now the only major cloud platform offering OpenAI and Anthropic frontier models alongside each other under a single procurement contract — a configuration that was unthinkable two years ago when OpenAI was effectively exclusive to Azure. Second, the first-party pricing parity removes the usual reason enterprises avoided multi-cloud LLM access: cross-cloud markup. Now the only reason to keep OpenAI workloads on Azure is workflow inertia, not cost.

This is the supply-side mirror of the consolidation story the feed covered earlier in the week with the four AI-lab acquisitions. Enterprises that built their stack around AWS no longer need to maintain a separate Azure tenancy for OpenAI, or a separate Anthropic billing relationship for Claude. The competitive pressure on Microsoft is real: when Microsoft also announced its in-house MAI-Thinking-1 and MAI-Code-1-Flash models at Build the same week, the implicit message was that Microsoft wants its own models running on Azure regardless of what OpenAI does about cross-cloud distribution.

For learners: pay attention to where models are hosted, not just which model is best on benchmarks. The cheapest-per-token frontier model your employer can actually procure is the one running on whatever cloud they already have a contract with. Multi-cloud access changes the math on which provider you build prototypes against — and increasingly, the decision is being made by procurement teams reading AWS Marketplace listings, not engineering teams reading model cards.
